_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Washington County, OH. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Washington County was formed in 1788 — the oldest county in both Ohio and the Northwest Territory. Exceptional early records from the Ohio Company settlement.

previous_courthouses.fate: The original seat of government for the Northwest Territory. Courts were held inside the fortified compound. Records from this period survive because the Ohio Company kept meticulous documentation.
Early courthouses were frame structures. No significant fire damage. Records transferred intact to successor buildings.
Brick courthouse served for nearly eighty years. Replaced by the current 1903 building. Records survived the transition intact.

record_losses.cause: No major fire, flood, or war damage
record_losses.description: Washington County has one of the most complete sets of early records in the state, owing to the Ohio Company's rigorous record-keeping tradition and the absence of catastrophic loss events. Records run continuously from the county's 1788 formation. The Ohio Company's own corporate records (land sales, surveys, meeting minutes) supplement the county records. Gaps in the genealogical record for Washington County are more likely to reflect the ancestor's circumstances than courthouse destruction.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Washington County's records go back to 1788 — the beginning of organized settlement in the Northwest Territory. If {ancestor_name} was in the county after that date, the paper trail almost certainly exists. Absence of records says something about the ancestor, not the courthouse.
record_losses.source: Ohio Historical Society. Guide to County Records in the Ohio Historical Society Archives.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous from county formation. Ohio Company land grants provide pre-county deed equivalents.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Early volumes show wear but remain legible. Many have been microfilmed.
deeds.repository: Washington County Recorder's Office; Ohio History Connection microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited Ancestry.com Ohio land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — probate records survive from near-formation
wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers from the 1790s–1810s are fragmentary, but bound volumes are complete.
wills_estates.repository: Washington County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection

marriages.completeness: Good — Ohio required marriage licenses from statehood (1803). Earlier records are less systematic but exist from the territorial period.
marriages.gaps: Some early territorial-period marriages were recorded only in church records. After 1803, county recording is continuous.
marriages.repository: Washington County Probate Court; Ohio History Connection microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — Northwest Territory court records survive from the very first session. Court of Common Pleas records from statehood.
court_minutes.gaps: Minimal. The territorial court records are among the oldest government records in the Old Northwest.
court_minutes.repository: Washington County Clerk of Courts; Ohio History Connection

vital_records.completeness: Ohio required vital registration beginning 1867, but compliance was uneven until the early twentieth century. Washington County's records are better than most due to Marietta's institutional culture.
vital_records.repository: Washington County Health Department; Ohio Department of Health

substitute_sources.description: The Ohio Company maintained detailed records of land sales, surveys, shareholders, and meeting minutes. These corporate records effectively function as pre-county land and settlement records.
substitute_sources.repository: Marietta College Special Collections; Ohio History Connection; Library of Congress (Rufus Putnam papers)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for identifying original settlers and their land holdings. The Ohio Company records name settlers, their origins (primarily New England), and their land purchases with a precision that county records of the era rarely match.
substitute_sources.source: Buell, Rowena. The Memoirs of Rufus Putnam. Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1903.

substitute_sources.description: Washington County appears in every federal census from 1790 to the present. The 1790 census for Washington County is the earliest census taken in what would become Ohio.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1790 census for Washington County is the baseline document for early settlement research. Manufacturing schedules (1820–1880) document early industries. Agricultural schedules detail farm operations.

substitute_sources.description: The New England settlers brought Congregational and Presbyterian traditions. Church records from the earliest period supplement civil records for births, marriages, and deaths.
substitute_sources.repository: Local congregations; Marietta College Special Collections; Ohio History Connection
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for the earliest settlement period when civil records were still being established. Church membership rolls and baptism records may be the only documentation of some early families.
substitute_sources.source: Hildreth, Samuel P. Pioneer History. Cincinnati: H. W. Derby, 1848.

narrative_hooks: Washington County's records begin in 1788 — the year the Ohio Company built Campus Martius and started writing everything down. If {ancestor_name} was in the county, the New England founders' obsession with documentation means the paper trail probably survives.

The Ohio Company kept better records than most county governments because they were a corporation with shareholders — every land sale, every survey, every meeting was documented with the same precision they had brought from New England.

If {ancestor_name} appears in the 1790 federal census for Washington County, {pronoun_subject} is documented in the oldest census taken anywhere in what would become Ohio — the baseline record of the Northwest Territory.

Gaps in Washington County's records are rare and meaningful — if {ancestor_name} does not appear, the absence likely reflects transience, poverty, or settlement in a part of the county too remote for the recorder to reach, not courthouse destruction.
source_references: Ohio Historical Society. Guide to County Records in the Ohio Historical Society Archives.
Buell, Rowena. The Memoirs of Rufus Putnam. Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1903.
Hildreth, Samuel P. Pioneer History. Cincinnati: H. W. Derby, 1848.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Washington County, OH.
